data.title: Keyera: Benefitting From Basin Growth
data.description:
Keyera Corp. (TSX:KEY:CA) wants to be the North American leader in delivering energy infrastructure solutions. I generally like energy infrastructure businesses because they often have high barrier to entry assets.

Company background

Keyera is based in Calgary, Canada. It is the smallest of the four main Canadian Oil and Gas Storage and Transportation stocks, the others being Enbridge Inc (ENB, ENB:CA), Pembina Pipeline Corp (PBA, PPL:CA), and TC Energy Corp (TRP, TRP:CA).

The company operates a fully integrated value chain centered around transporting, processing, and marketing Natural Gas Liquids, NGLs.

Keyera has three operating segments: Gathering and Processing; Liquids Infrastructure; and Marketing. In financial reporting, Keyera often groups Gathering and Processing and Liquids Infrastructure together under the name Fee-for-Service Segments. In the last three years, realized margin (which is operating margin excluding unrealized gains and losses from commodity related risk management contracts) averaged 66% for Fee-for-Service and 34% for Marketing, as a percent of total realized margin.

The fee-for-service is renting volume to third parties for its storage and transportation assets, while the marking business "markets a range of products associated with its two infrastructure business lines, primarily propane, butane, condensate and iso-octane, and also engages in liquids blending activities." In other words marketing expose Keyera to commodity market price risks, which it looks to manage.

A detailed description of the business can be found on its Annual Information Form, year ending December 31, 2024.

The following map shows Keyera’s principal operations in Canada.

Financial analysis and critical factors

Keyera, Pembina and TC Energy all trade with a trailing P/E of around 20x. Enbridge which is one of the world's largest energy companies, has a trailing P/E of 24x, I believe because of a more attractive growth outlook, which was set out in its 2025 Investor Day presentation.

Keyera's last Investor Day was in 2022, so I'll focus on more recent data, but the Investor Day presentation did highlight a couple of things I will come back to: KAPS, which it called "A Game Changer" and a Strategic Relationship With Shell.

Normally when I look at a new company, in the U.S. I will go to the latest 10-K, and in Europe, I will go to the Annual Report. In Canada there is no one document. Instead we must go to the Annual Information Form, audited Annual Financial Statements, and the Management Discussion and Analysis. At Keyera, the MD&A and financial statements can be found in its Year-End Report.

In terms of trying to understand the critical factors that will drive Keyera's earnings, let's take a look at the assets and metrics of each segment that are going to dictate financial performance.

Gathering and Processing

According to the 2024 Management’s Discussion and Analysis:

Keyera currently has interests in 9 active gas plants, all of which are located in Alberta. Keyera operates 7 of the 9 active gas plants. The Gathering and Processing segment includes raw gas gathering systems and processing plants strategically located in the natural gas production areas on the western side of the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin (“WCSB”).

Net processing throughput measured in million standard cubic feet per day, or MMcf/d, which refers to Keyera’s share of raw gas processed at its processing facilities, is the key metric here. In 2024 this metric was down 3% over 2023. However, in the first half of of 2025, this metric was up 7% vs the same period in 2024. Likely to a major extent the underlying exposure is natural gas production on the western side of the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin.

Liquids Infrastructure

According to the 2024 Management’s Discussion and Analysis:

The Liquids Infrastructure segment provides fractionation, storage, transportation, liquids blending and terminaling services for NGLs and crude oil, and produces iso-octane. These services are provided to customers through an extensive network of facilities, including the following assets:

  • NGL and condensate pipelines
  • Underground NGL storage caverns
  • Above ground storage tanks
  • NGL fractionation and de-ethanization facilities
  • Pipeline, rail and truck terminals
  • Liquids blending facilities
  • The AEF facility (Alberta EnviroFuels).

Net processing throughput, measured in thousand barrels per day, or Mbbl/d, is the key measure here, along with the AEF iso-octane production volumes also measured in Mbbl/d. In 2024 net processing throughput in the Liquids Infrastructure segment was down 4% over 2023 and AEF iso-octane production volumes were down 13% in 2024 vs 2023. In the first half of 2025, net processing throughput was up 4% vs H1 2024, while AEF iso-octane production volumes were down 17% period on period.

NGLs, condensate and iso-octane have various demand drivers including oil sands diluent, gasoline blending, petrochemicals, residential heating, so it is difficult to narrow this segment down to one core demand driver, while the supply driver is coming from condensate and NGL production levels.

Capacity utilization of transportation and storage assets would be an important metric, but as yet I could not find detailed information on this, reported by the company.

Take-or-Pay

In the company's Fee-for-Service business, in 2024 52% of the realized margin, came from Take-or-Pay contracts. Take-or-Pay provides financial protection when a customer no longer needs booked capacity.

Marketing

Marketing is largely a function of sales volumes measured in barrels per day, or Bbl/d, and the spread between purchase and sales prices. This is essentially an arbitrage business exploiting price differentials between point A and point B, or between time A and time B, utilizing storage. Keyera also engages in blending products of lower value into higher value products. Marketing is benefits from integration with Keyera's Gathering and Processing and Liquids Infrastructure assets (hence the inter-segment eliminations).

The 2024 Management’s Discussion and Analysis also notes:

In a typical year, Keyera expects the Marketing business to contribute on average, a base realized margin of between $310 million and $350 million. This guidance assumes: i) a crude oil price of between US$65 and US$75 per barrel; ii) butane feedstock costs comparable to the 10-year average; and iii) AEF utilization at nameplate capacity.

With good risk management, Keyera would benefit from volatile markets.

Growth opportunities

The macro backdrop is a bit mixed: Power generation and industrial use should support natural gas, I expect petrochemical demand growth too. Gasoline blending and oil sands may face longer term headwinds. However NGLs are low in carbon content, so they will be used to bring the carbon intensity down of other oil products.

However, with Canadian West Coast LNG exports set to ramp, with LNG Canada online, and Woodfibre LNG and LNG Canada Phase II announced and proposed proposed respectively, it would not surprise me if natural gas production in the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin were to ramp up to support gas flows for export.

Specifically, Keyera is expected to benefit from condensate and NGL production growth from the Montney and Duvernay basins in Western Canada.

The Key Access Pipeline System known as KAPS is the largest project in Keyera’s history. Completed in 2023 it is 50/50 joint venture with Stonepeak, which is "a leading alternative investment firm specializing in essential infrastructure." Keyera operates the pipeline, and an expansion project known as KAPS Zone 4 has been sanctioned and expected to come online mid-2027.

Another interesting project, signed in 2022 is the strategic relationship with Shell, looking to build a gathering and distribution network to transport captured CO2 from Keyera and other operations in Alberta’s "Industrial Heartland" to Shell Canada’s Atlas Carbon Storage Hub. In June 2024, Shell announced Final Investment Decision to proceed with the Atlas Carbon Storage Hub.

Acquisition of Plains’ Canadian NGL Business

More recently, Keyera announced the "Transformative" acquisition of Plains' Canadian NGL business. This will go into the Liquids Infrastructure business. Seeking Alpha author Trapping Value called this a Gift and a game changer - so I won't cover the deal in detail. The deal is expected to close in Q1 2026, subject to regulatory approvals.

However I do want to share a nice macro chart from its deal presentation.

Risks

So far so good. Keyera is another way to play the natural gas story and there appears to be volume growth, so maybe its P/E can rerate from 20x to closer to Enbridge's 24x.

So what challenges could the company face?

First its supply of diluent and solvents Canada's Oil Sands industry, could be problematic. Oil Sands are very high cost production, and if crude prices are not high, Oil Sands quickly become unprofitable which in theory would reduce production. This could negatively impact Keyera's Liquids Infrastructure segment.

As the company states in the 2024 Notes to annual consolidated financial statements:

Most of Keyera’s Liquids Infrastructure assets are located in, or connected to, the Edmonton/Fort Saskatchewan area in Alberta, one of four key energy hubs in North America. This area also serves as a condensate hub which supports the operations of customers in the oil sands sector. Condensate is used as a diluent to facilitate movement of bitumen by pipeline.

Then there is its Iso-octane business. This goes into motor gasoline, or more specifically Reformulated Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending or “RBOB.” If the Canadian government moves against internal combustion engine, then the demand for Iso-octane will fall.

Then there is the commodity price risk that can be seen in the spread between reported Operating margin and Realized margin. The difference is "unrealized loss on risk management contracts". In 2024, this spread appeared to be small, but it just takes one rogue trader to create large losses.

In terms of financial risk, it's Altman Z-Score is just 1.63 which is quite weak.

Old economy

Keyera's stock on the TSX is up 131% over the last five years. Not bad. Most of the assets of the company are coming from Property, plant and equipment. Modern value investors are looking at both tangible and intangible assets. So does Keyera have any intangible value?

For a start there is the high barriers to entry in the physical infrastructure system it has built. This is the main point.

What about technological developments and digital transformation? In its 2024 Annual Information Form, Keyera discusses this as a risk more than an opportunity. It says failure to stay on top of technological developments and digital transformation could "materially adversely affect Keyera’s operations and its ability to compete in the market." I can give credit to Keyera for not AI-washing its presentations. But it could be an opportunity as AI start-ups like Applied Computing bring innovation to the energy industry, especially the processing heavy. But for now, Keyera's main and important intangible comes from its attractive geographic positioning.

Valuation

The 20x trailing P/E is historically not cheap or expensive. But with the growth opportunities on the table, if Keyera can execute, it could rerate several points higher.

Conclusion

Keyera's guidance of Fee-Based Adjusted EBITDA CAGR of 7-8% from 2024 through 2027 seems easily doable. With a supportive macro backdrop and what looks to be a robust business the stock looks to be worth owning, as I look to add more natural gas or natural gas related exposure to my portfolio. Now I have introduced myself to the company, I know what to watch for and I'll be watching this stock closely.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Power Solutions International: A Data Center Rerating That Looks Overdone
data.description:
Power Solutions International, Inc. (NASDAQ:PSIX) is a classic overdone rerating story. The business has successfully pivoted to AI infrastructure and data center power systems. The fundamentals have improved and a visible turnaround in financials does deserve an upgrade. But there are still several risks on the table that are being overlooked in a classic momentum-driven trade, as PSIX rallies to new highs every day. The overall case for PSIX is that of a good business, not necessarily a good investment at these prices.

The Pivot: Industrial Engines to Data Center

PSIX is a traditional industrial designer and manufacturer of emission-tested engines and power systems. Its client base spans across industrials, power systems and transportation. PSIX has a significant track record of validated product fit, with its fuel-agnostic engines going through years of manufacturing, improvements, and sales. The strategic pivot that has caught the attention of the market is the power systems segment that has now seen traction in data center's power generation use cases.

Fundamental Indicators Solid Only on Surface

Let us look at some narrative-altering indicators and dig a little deeper into what they signal. Revenues have seemingly seen a breakout quarter in Q2 2025, with a ~74% YoY jump in topline. This is yet to be a story of sustained high growth, because if you remove the last quarter's uptick in revenues, the story is not one of consistent growth over time.

Q2 presents a similar breakthrough story for net income, up ~138% YoY. Again, a clear breakthrough pattern, but not backed by consistent performance across a few quarters. In fact, double-clicking on the income improvement reveals a close to $29.2m net income increase attributable to the release of valuation allowance on deferred tax assets, almost the entire increase in net income. This is not a breakthrough earnings quarter but rather a one-time accounting adjustment. The best news that can be derived from this is the resolution of going concern doubts and progress on debt management—notably a refinancing extending debt maturity to 2027.

Another factor that should keep the excitement in check is the explosion in SG&A by ~269% YoY to fund the revenue expansion. This is not great for PSIX's immediate operating leverage and also shows the revenue increase has not been simply an unaided demand uptick but more concerted growth efforts in sales and marketing—expected in the growth phase but puts the revenue explosion in better perspective.

Gross margins have also marginally contracted in Q2 through the scaling phase. While it's still the initial days of the pivot to AI and data center-led demand, gross margin pressures surely signal that there are several validations pending—notably how well PSIX does in the face of competition and its impact on pricing power.

Overall, the only silver lining in Q2 beyond headline income improvements is the quality of the revenue mix. While legacy demand remained stagnant, the power systems segment drove the entire revenue uptick, attributable to the data center use case. This is definitely an encouraging development and a solid rerating catalyst, but there are many questions still unanswered, primarily around sustained demand signals beyond just a quarter.

Valuations Stretched for Stage

PSIX's EV to forward Sales ratio of ~4.3x is extremely high for an industrials small cap. It makes the expectations of continued and uninterrupted hypergrowth too high and is built into share prices today. Where the reality is that apart from an encouraging foray into AI and data center demand, there are still many unanswered questions. Competitive and technology risks, or even pricing power remain open questions that are yet to be validated. A far bigger and more diversified player like Vertiv (with almost 10x revenue scale) commands a ~5.5x EV to forward Sales ratio. Vertiv's positioning is that of a far more mature business with lower execution risks and a somewhat proven competitive positioning. Of course, PSIX's valuations, if the hypergrowth continues, could still be justified. A slightly lower valuation with a superior topline growth profile should be palatable, but I will need to see that hypergrowth translating at the expected pace and without damaging other open questions like gross margins to become a believer.

To understand the valuations better, it is important to understand PSIX's competitive positioning. PSIX finds itself in a fragmented but highly competitive space. It does have some moat-like defense characteristics as seen in its fuel-agnostic tech, emission-friendly products and a customizable solution that can cater to specific client needs.

However, when it comes to the data center power markets, there is intense competition from bigger players who have already made significant inroads. Several research studies indicate varying numbers of market shares of the leaders in thermal management providers for data centers. Most of them definitely agree that Vertiv, Schneider and Daikin (and a few others) are market leaders in the segment. Even dated data on the data center cooling market in 2019-20 shows Vertiv as commanding ~23.5% of the total market. Which means the combined leadership of the top players accounts for the vast majority of the market and additionally has years of relationships and proven track records at scale. That not only highlights the magnitude of the challenge that PSIX faces to make continued inroads against established incumbents but also shows why PSIX's current valuations do not account for the difference in scale and execution risks compared to Vertiv.

DCF Model Shows PSIX is Overpriced

The valuations are stretched but still palatable under certain assumptions like a continued hypergrowth and a narrative premium due to AI and data center demand. But any hopes of even a Hold thesis are quashed by my conservative DCF model.

I have built the DCF model on a 5-year horizon keeping the tech and execution risks in mind. PSIX is still a small-cap, potentially rapid growth company in a fast-evolving sector—longer forecast periods will carry too much uncertainty. A realistic growth trajectory starting with a 30% topline growth in Year 1 and slowing down to around say 8% in 5 years (at sizeable revenue levels of ~$1.3b annually) is assumed. This growth profile respects the realities of competitive pressures and moderating growth on a larger base later on.

The FCF margins assumption acknowledges reduction in R&D and SG&A spends only over time as PSIX scales. A higher WACC of ~14% is taken to reflect small-cap risk premium and execution risks. This realistic model, which I would say is not overly pessimistic, shows an intrinsic value of $56 for PSIX, far below the current levels of well beyond $100.

I reverse checked what scenario is built into today's share prices of around $110. The current prices expect a ~70% growth in Year 1 (in line with the Q2 YoY growth seen). The topline growth then slows to ~40-50% in Years 2-3 and 25-30% in the last 2 years. This is almost an ask that ignores the lumpy nature of hyperscaler contracts and sales cycles and expects continued top-class execution amidst low competition. The FCF assumptions for today's prices should be ~16% margins in Year 1 and expanding to ~21% in Year 5, which assumes huge efficiencies right from the start. Even the terminal growth rate should be ~3% (instead of 2% in our realistic model) and a discount rate of ~12%. That is valuing PSIX almost like a less risky mid-cap. That is also a very high bar!

Management Guidance and Reality Check

Management commentary in Q2 2025 is optimistic on continued inroads in data center products, while the traditional business is expected to remain flat through 2025. There are no specific guidance targets on the growth levels as management quoted geopolitical uncertainties and tariff impacts as areas of caution.

Alongside greater macroeconomic uncertainties, there are other execution risks that the management is focusing on—notably streamlining manufacturing efforts and scaling manufacturing capacity to meet the increase in demand.

But Can't Sell

Having seen the peer valuations and DCF assumptions, the prices are looking inflated, but not as inflated as in some other narrative-based stocks in today's AI and data center-led optimism. Looking through the headline numbers, all is not rosy or proven yet, but the core story of expansion into AI and data centers is valid and worth the rerating. I feel data points to an overdone rerating and PSIX being a candidate for a Sell. But the gap between intrinsic value and share prices can be bridged with a couple of quarters of continued hypergrowth. Shorting PSIX, although quantitatively indicated, is not practically recommended.

I would at best encourage buying risk-contained put positions far dated and out of the money as a speculative call for action from the almost bearish thesis. But a Sell rating looks too bold, particularly given the fact that the narrative is real and several other stocks have ridden the momentum to far greater disparities from fundamentally indicated levels. So, I remain bearish on PSIX, rate it a Hold, and wait for any correction below at least ~$80 to be interested in longs.

data.title: Hershey: Premium Valuation Despite Significant Macro Issues, But Worth Watching
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

The Hershey Company (NYSE:HSY) is the #1 US confectionery manufacturer, with an expanding presence in non-chocolate snacks and some of the most popular sweets brands on the planet, including Reese's, Payday, Almond Joy, Kit Kat (US only), and of course, their flagship brand, Hershey's. Right now, the company is navigating through some tough times for the broader food industry, but some significant catalysts are developing, while their new CEO could offer potential for an accelerated recovery.

Their latest reports have been marked by a very significant drop in net income due to higher cost of sales (will mention below), while the cash from operations also dropped, especially due to working capital changes. Although these are very easily explained by macro changes, this created very significant volatility in the stock price over the past year, although they have recovered and now trade at basically the same price as a year ago. They're also expecting a drop in CAPEX to ~$425 million to $450 million, which is about $150 million to $175 million less than a year ago.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that they are in an okay position, with the current assets well above the current liabilities and quite a bit of long-term debt. We see a ~$2 billion increase in long-term debt, although note that the short-term debt decreased by ~$1.14 billion as a result of the company issuing $2 billion back in February to refinance all of its outstanding 0.9% Senior Notes due 2025 and 3.2% Senior Notes due 2025. These are spread into four tranches: one in 2028 (4.55% interest rate), 2030 (4.75%), 2032 (4.95%), and 2035 (5.1%). This should add quite a bit of interest (about $76.25 million), which is definitely not a small amount given their cash flow, making them increase their interest expense guidance this year, as can be seen below.

Now, they expect $170 million to $180 million in tariff expenses combined with a massive drop in EPS of ~50% due to a combination of derivative mark-to-market gain back in 2024's reported earnings, a non-cash gain that won't repeat, combined of course with the price of cocoa even tripling due to West Africa's poor harvest levels and tariffs. I don't believe these are permanent issues, but it's also worth noting that the company's stock is still performing very well compared to peers, despite being affected by the same industry headwinds - or even more due to their dependency on chocolate.

Dividends

The company's dividend isn't really amazing despite the recent increase in yield, representing about $1.11 billion, which is a lot for the company in its current state. This shouldn't be an issue now, but if the macro uncertainty mentioned below continues to hurt their bottom line and volumes, this might have to be cut - and it's not like it was a significant yield anyway.

Risks & Opportunities

A point mentioned in previous articles is that the industry's (and consequently the company's) growth ultimately depends on the economic cycle and consumers' discretionary budgets. Virtually every company that depended on the consumer - such as Nike (NKE), PepsiCo (PEP), and many others - has reported issues in consumer demand, especially in North America and China, where most of them reported flat or even declining sales. In Hershey's case, this hasn't been a huge negative so far, as the company still managed to grow its revenue a bit and remain at relatively flat levels in terms of cash flow, but it definitely had an impact.

A major improvement that I believe should be their number one opportunity in the near term will be the effect of rate cuts, with two more cuts expected this year after the one in September. By depending on the discretionary budgets, and since they've been affected by a weaker consumer environment, I believe rate cuts could play an important role in a recovery when it comes to discretionary demand.

By lowering interest rates, the Fed basically increases discretionary budgets, which could be great for the company's consumers and the company itself, as that would lower interest rate payments for both sides. This can be great for HSY as well, since their interest expenses got to nearly $200 million over the past 12 months. This isn't really a debt-ridden company, but it can help regardless, especially since it would allow them to expand at lower rates in case they want to.

With their nearly 3% yield, it's also worth noting that rate cuts will be great for dividend stocks as well. Rate cuts have the effect of lowering treasury yields and make these stocks more attractive, as that money would seek better yields, potentially outside of the US as well. As mentioned before, I believe high rates and the resulting high treasury yields were a very significant factor when it comes to the overall weak performance of food stocks since the pandemic.

Another thing I want to note is that Hershey's products aren't really the most defensive. The same thing I said about Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) in the recent article: when people have to tighten the belt, sweets should be one of the first steps they take. Somewhat related, another thing that's been visible for many industries is a de-premiumization effect due to lower discretionary budgets or higher prices (both, in this case), which is visible in these companies' volumes. We've seen this in recent years as many companies in the industry started reporting issues, including General Mills (GIS), which I analyzed recently and rated a Buy thanks to their overall high recovery potential.

On top of that, Hershey can be significantly affected by the current administration's fight against the industry, especially the products full of sugar and others. As HSY focuses on sweets, this impact can be significantly worse than it was for other players that I covered before, such as General Mills or Nestlé. They are focusing on better-for-you brands, but that doesn't mean there won't be any impact from current or especially future Make America Healthy Again policies. It remains an uncertainty to watch for now.

Trump's tariffs will also continue to play a very important role in the near future, and we've seen HSY already fall over 8% back when they trimmed the FY 2026 guidance and cocoa futures jumped 11%. Given the unpredictable nature of tariffs, simply increasing the costs to try and mitigate these is not necessarily the best idea (most companies did/will do this, including Hershey), as it can lead to worse volumes and still hurt the company's bottom line, while taking the hit and waiting for commodity prices to normalize can be a risky gamble, so they are caught in a tough spot. Regarding tariffs, this was their former CEO's response during the latest Q&A (this was before the 11% jump):

As we got hit by some of the record high cocoa prices early on, we stated that our approach was going to be taking a long-term approach to ensure continued category health, and we've done that. We've continued to spend on our brands, we've invested in technology with our new ERP platform, and then new AI and tech-enabled capabilities that have driven significant efficiency, whether in the transformation program or other places.

As a note, the company has had a new CEO since August, with a relatively good history as the CEO of Wendy's (WEN) and over 30 years of work at PepsiCo. He seems to be on the same track as the previous CEO, saying that he's committed to continuing the company's vision to be a major player in the broader snacking category, building on what the previous CEO started. This includes their expansion into non-chocolate snacks and managing the current industry challenges while also potentially focusing more on global expansion, which is an opportunity for Hershey as they are quite weak outside of North America.

Still, we are likely to see them keep trying to expand, which I think is a good move given their stock's valuation compared to peers. In fact, we've seen HSY expand through acquisitions like LesserEvil (announced in April 2025; ~$750 million, better-for-you snacks), Actual Candy in 2024, two facilities from Weaver Popcorn Manufacturing Facilities in 2023, and more.

However, Hershey itself could also be the target of an acquisition, with Mondelez (MDLZ) trying to acquire HSY last year, but the latter refused the bid, saying it was too low. This wasn't even their first attempt, as it was also tried before back in 2016. I believe such a deal (which would be breaking records given the size) could make sense for a more diversified player like Mondelez or PepsiCo, which would gain significant US exposure and be able to withstand industry headwinds such as the volatile cocoa prices, which is tougher for Hershey alone. Not only that, but such a giant could expand Hershey's brands internationally, benefitting from their massive logistics networks, which Hershey lacks internationally.

But another thing to note here is that the Hershey Trust owns most of the company's voting power through Class B shares, with about 78% of voting power based on their latest AGM. And even if the Trust would approve it, the regulatory uncertainty behind such a massive deal would remain tough.

Peer Comparison

However, when we get to the peer comparison, this is when HSY starts to look weak. Comparing them to local and global competitors such as Kellanova (K), General Mills, Nestlé, Mondelez, Chocoladefabriken Lindt & Sprüngli (OTCPK:LDSVF), and The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM), we see not only the lowest dividend yield but also some of the highest valuation metrics from many points of view. In an environment with issues where virtually all players are hurt and trading at distressed valuations, this company still comes with a very high valuation, and keep in mind that most of these are global players that depend on much more than just the US/North America (vs. ~90% North America for HSY).

Right now, we're paying a premium price for something that's not really worth that much, with a lot of competition that's trading at much better ratios. But sure, a recovery in the industry should be a recovery for HSY as well, although I believe their potential is significantly lower than that of peers such as GIS, both in terms of stock price appreciation and dividends.

Valuation

Based on H1 2025, seasonality in H2, and their CAPEX expectations, I'll estimate that they can make around $850 million in FCF this year. I'll expect some improvements in the environment and the company getting back to $1 billion in 2026 and back to ~$1.25 billion in 2027, followed by a continued solid 7.5% CAGR until 2030 thanks to their expansion potential, rate cuts, and an overall better environment (note: this is below their five-year EPS CAGR of ~15.5%, for example, to take into account the business maturing plus competition adapting to the environment), followed by a 4.5% CAGR for the subsequent five years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. With $4.73 billion in net debt based on the latest report, 202.7 million shares outstanding, and a WACC of 7% (above my estimated 6.62%, based on a cost of equity of ~7.1% and cost of debt of ~4.7% for a better margin of safety), this returns an enterprise value of $31.12 billion. After subtracting net debt, this gives an equity value of $26.39 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of ~$130.21.

Conclusions

Overall, although I see potential in Hershey to recover together with the broader food industry, I rate them a Hold as a result of the valuation with high expectations and dependency on the North American market and chocolate. These are tied significantly to politics, with both internal issues (Make America Healthy Again policies) and external factors (tariffs) being possible headwinds, although the industry's tailwinds and the company's potential to expand globally remain attractive.

data.title: Kodiak AI Not Worth Investing At This Time
data.description:
Kodiak AI Inc. (NASDAQ:KDK) has begun trading on the Nasdaq after Ares Acquisition Corp. II shareholders approved its SPAC deal valued at $2.5 billion. As I write articles on autonomous vehicles for SA, I feel compelled to weigh in on this new stock.

Is it worth investing? Early on the first day of trading, the stock was down more than 16%, and it had declined by almost 5% by Friday morning. Here's why I would pass on this at the current time.

Reportedly, there was a lack of confidence in the deal at that valuation from many of the Ares Acquisition Corp. II investors. Article:

Kodiak’s road to Wall Street nearly hit a dead end amid a rush of shareholder redemptions.

Ahead of Tuesday’s vote, several of the SPAC’s investors took advantage of their right to redeem their shares, rather than become a shareholder of the combined company. This action drained Ares Acquisition Corp. II’s trust account, which Kodiak previously noted had $562 million in cash as of Aug. 18.

But after heavy redemptions — despite raising an additional $275 million — there was only $62.9 million remaining in the trust account before expenses, Ares disclosed.

Although Kodiak is not receiving as much funding as it could have from the trust, the company said Tuesday that institutional investors have funded or committed additional money of over $200 million.

The market opportunity for autonomous long-haul trucking is tremendous. The benefits are significant, and the savings to truck operators will make it a compelling option. (Autonomous Vehicles: Opportunities, Strategies, and Disruptions). The challenge is the time and capital required to launch it into the market. Many companies, such as Embark and TuSimple, have failed. I think Kodiak will face challenges.

In its Investor Presentation, Kodiak discloses:

  • It currently has four autonomous trucks in operation, with Atlas Energy Solutions working on private construction roads.
  • It has driven 2.8 million autonomous miles and delivered more than 7,300 loads.
  • It is testing with Werner Enterprises and J.B. Hunt.
  • It also has projects with the Department of Defense.
  • It plans to have hundreds of trucks operating in 2026 and thousands in 2027.
  • It claims a quarterly burn rate of approximately $20 million.

The Kodiak Driver (its autonomous driving platform) is well constructed and tested. It was developed and designed primarily for Class 8 trucks, but it isn’t limited to any specific truck. Like many other autonomous driving systems, it employs modular sensors, including cameras, radar, and lidar. It features redundant systems for power, steering, and braking. Kodiak uses a Vehicle Actuation Control Engine to manage actuation and enforce safety constraints.

Its software system uses AI to interpret the truck's environment, detecting vehicles, obstacles, lane signs, and other relevant objects. Then, its planning models predict the actions of different cars. By using sensors to look ahead, it then determines the route decisions for the truck.

However, unlike some others, the Kodiak Driver does not utilize HD maps, such as those used by Waymo. HD maps incorporate essential information (effectively metadata) such as road geometry, traffic and signage data, crossing and protected area identification, and static landmarks for localization. Instead, Kodiak uses what it calls “sparse maps,” which contain less detailed information necessary for highway driving. This makes them easier to maintain and reduces processing needs. Its system relies more on perception than localization, which may be adequate for highway driving.

Kodiak has tested the Kodiak drive for over 2.8 million miles.

Kodiak's business model (see below) is a driver-as-a-service model, where it charges a per-mile license fee of $0.97, less the estimated customer savings of 15%-35%, or a monthly/annual license fee of an estimated $ 341,000, less the customer savings.

For the license fee, Kodiak provides an autonomous system (hardware and software) as well as operational and remote support services, but not the truck itself. It claims to have a long-term gross margin target of 60%-80%, but I believe that will take many years to achieve, if at all possible.

Using its revenue model and the estimated number of trucks operational in 2026 and 2027, it would have the following range of revenue:

  • 2026: 200-400 trucks would generate $60-$120 million.
  • 2027: 2,000 trucks would generate $600 million.

Based on the experiences of others, I find their autonomous truck ramp plan to be somewhat optimistic.

Kodiak based its initial valuation to a large extent on a comparison to Aurora Innovation, Inc. (AUR), which has a market cap of more than $10 billion. Aurora is already on the road in Texas, hauling loads for FedEx on the busy Interstate 45 corridor between Dallas and Houston without a safety driver. (ABC News). While it has a higher burn rate than Kodiak, it has more than $1 billion in cash.

Overall, I believe that Kodiak is too risky an investment at this time. Its autonomous technology may work very well, but it will most likely require more capital before it can sustain a breakeven operation. It may also find it challenging to raise the necessary capital. Although it's more expensive, I believe Aurora would be a better long-term investment. I previously bought shares of Ares Acquisition Corp. II but sold them.

On the positive side, the market opportunity for autonomous long-haul trucking is substantial, and Kodiak could reach its rollout with sufficient capital or raise the additional funding needed. The market will likely support several competitors, so keep an eye on Kodiak's progress.

data.title: Corcept Primed For An Upside EPS Surprise
data.description:
Corcept Therapeutics Incorporated (NASDAQ:CORT) shares have been strong throughout this year even as the company has delivered financial results below expectations. What has led to a 64% increase in the stock this year are positive trial results, the FDA accepting a second New Drug Application in less than a year, what appears to be a resolution to its single-source pharmacy problem and multiple medical conference presentations on the company’s million plus patient opportunity for hard-to-treat diabetes that confirm the huge growth opportunity for the company.

An example of intense interest from the medical community comes from Sean Maduck, Corcept’s President of Endocrinology, saying on the June quarter conference call, “A real-life example is at the Endo conference about 3 weeks ago here in San Francisco, the Endo Society Conference, we had a presentation on the CATALYST data (hard-to-treat diabetes) in a room that had seats for about 225 people. 500 people attended that session. There were more people standing than sitting. And that would have been absolutely unheard of 2 years ago.”

The next step is to show strong revenue growth, increased margins and what I believe will be EPS coming in above expectations. The shares can then continue to materially outperform the market and not be subject to the various headwinds the economy is facing.

Avoided the “penalty box” while missing analyst projections

Even though Corcept doesn’t give quarterly revenue or earnings guidance it has missed expectations for the past three quarters due to its single-source pharmacy being overwhelmed with new prescriptions. It has fared better on reported EPS, but earnings have been materially helped in the first and second quarters this year by tax credits vs. owing the government money. For the past three quarters:

December 2024 quarter

  • Revenue of $182 million vs. projection of $200 million
  • EPS of $0.26 vs. analyst’s projection of $0.42
  • A lower tax rate of 3.8% helped EPS by $0.03
  • $10 million of inventory build in R&D expense hurt EPS by $0.08
  • $18 million in additional revenue would have generated about $0.09 in EPS
  • Including all three impacts EPS would have been $0.40, just below expectations

March 2025 quarter

  • Revenue of $157 million vs. projection of $178 million
  • Reported EPS of $0.17 vs. projection of $0.14
  • Without the tax credit and a 15% tax rate EPS would have been $0.07
  • $21 million in additional revenue would have generated about $0.10 in EPS
  • EPS would have been $0.20, beating the $0.14 expectation

June 2025 quarter

  • Revenue of $194 million vs. projection of $199 million
  • Reported EPS of $0.29 vs. projection of $0.19
  • Without the tax credit and a 15% tax rate EPS would have been $0.22
  • Add $5 million of revenue and EPS would have been $0.25, again beating analysts projections

Significant earnings beat could be in the offing

There are four sell-side analysts covering Corcept. Their September quarter revenue and EPS projections are:

Revenue

Corcept had its largest revenue quarter in the company’s history last quarter generating $194.4 million, an increase of $37.2 million from the March quarter and 19% year-over-year growth. For the September quarter the analysts’ expectations are:

  • Low: $210 million (up $15.6 million quarter to quarter and growth of 15% year-over-year)
  • Average: $224 million (up $29.6 million and growth of 23%)
  • C. Jones: $225 million (up $30.6 million and growth of 23%)
  • High: $240 million (up $45.6 million and growth of 31%)

There is a large range probably due to the unknown of how much of June quarter’s $37.2 million quarterly increase was driven by the single source pharmacy catching up vs. new prescriptions.

EPS

My EPS estimate matches the highest projection of the four sell-side analysts and is significantly above the average estimate, even when the lowest projection of $0.03 is removed.

  • Low: $0.03
  • Average: $0.17 ($0.22 w/o low estimate of $0.03)
  • High: $0.29
  • C. Jones: $0.29

Since my revenue estimates are very close to the average analyst’s projection and the company has gross margins of just over 98%, the reason my EPS estimate is significantly higher than the analyst’s average estimate must be due to a difference in operating expenses.

Research & Development expenses have been flat

Research and Development expenses have essentially been flat from the first quarter of 2024 through the June 2025 quarter, only increasing 3.4%. One of the reasons is from the company’s lower activity in its ALS program that was completed in early 2024. As new trials and studies are initiated, I would not be surprised to see R&D spending start to ramp.

  • 1Q 2024: $58.5 million
  • 2Q 2024: $58.7 million (up $0.2 million quarter to quarter)
  • 3Q 2024: $59.3 million (up $0.6 million)
  • 4Q 2024: $70.3 million (includes about $10 million Relacorilant inventory build. Without up $1.0 million)
  • 1Q 2025: $60.7 million (up $0.4 million without inventory build)
  • 2Q 2025: $60.5 million (down $0.2 million)
  • 3Q 2025: $65.0 million estimate (up $4.5 million)

My $65 million estimate versus the last 5 quarters average increase of $0.4 million is based on detailed analysis of 10-Q and 10-K R&D expenditures where the oncology, Cushing’s and metabolic diseases segments have all increased over the past two quarters.

SG&A expenses steadily increasing

Sales, general and administrative expenses have been on a steady upwards trend as Corcept has significantly increased its endocrinology salesforce and intensified its marketing efforts surrounding the CATALYST study findings on hard-to-treat diabetes. Building out its oncology salesforce and support staff has also started and should help to continue a steady climb in this cost.

  • 1Q 2024: $56.3 million
  • 2Q 2024: $66.9 million (up $10.6 million quarter to quarter)
  • 3Q 2024: $73.7 million (up $6.8 million)
  • 4Q 2024: $83.4 million (up $9.7 million)
  • 1Q 2025: $90.7 million (up $7.3 million)
  • 2Q 2025: $103.9 million (up $13.2 million)
  • 3Q 2025: $119 million estimate (up $15.1 million)

After a significant increase in the June quarter’s SG&A expense vs. the March quarter, it seems appropriate to see another step up in this segments cost, especially since new salespeople are being hired but not fully productive.

Assumptions to calculate EPS

Since gross margins are approximately 98% a dollar change in revenue or operating expenses essentially impacts EPS by the same amount, positively or negatively.

For additional revenue I’m assuming that 15% could be needed for sales commissions.

Interest income has fallen due to Corcept buying back shares and lower interest rates. I am assuming this amount stays steady at $5 million per quarter as the company generates cash that helps to offset lower interest rates. While share buybacks would lower interest income, there is an offsetting benefit in a lower share count.

From 2022 to 2024 the company’s tax rate had fluctuated between 13% to 15% on an annual basis. I am using 15% to account for any taxes owed.

The diluted share count tends to increase around 1 million to 1.5 million shares per quarter with a large portion due to the increased share price impacting the calculation used to determine the number. I have assumed a 1.5 million increase to 122 million for the September quarter compared to a 666,000 increase in the June quarter.

How much EPS increases with higher revenue or lower expenses

Using the assumptions outlined above each incremental amount of revenue or lower expenses delivers a significant amount of profit.

Every $1 million in revenue generates $708,000 in net profit.

Every $1 million in lower or higher expenses impacts net profit by $850,000.

  • $1 million = $0.006 for revenue and $0.007 for expenses
  • $2 million = $0.012 for revenue and $0.014 for expenses
  • $3 million = $0.017 for revenue and $0.021 for expenses
  • $4 million = $0.023 for revenue and $0.028 for expenses
  • $5 million = $0.029 for revenue and $0.035 for expenses

My $0.29 EPS estimate vs. analyst’s $0.17 sensitivity analysis

If the EPS delta is to show up in lower revenue the delta will be approximately $25 million lower than the $225 million I am projecting or $200 million. This would be less than the lowest analyst estimate of $210 million and barely $5 million more than the June quarter’s $194.4 million.

To calculate how much higher Corcept’s operating expenses will have to be vs. my projection the difference will have to be $17 million using the $0.007 incremental hit to EPS. Since I have increased R&D spending significantly quarter-to-quarter and incrementally increased SG&A spending, the company will have to report much larger increases than I am forecasting.

Of course, there could be a combination of lower revenue and higher than expected expenses, but from the numbers I have laid out I believe this has low odds of occurring.

Major risks revolve around Relacorilant being approved and Teva being successful with its lawsuits or generic version

There are two major risks to the shares both revolving around Relacorilant, Corcept’s follow on drug to Korlym, not being approved by the FDA as it is always an unknown when and if a new drug will be given the thumps up. At least in this instance Corcept seems to have built a very strong case for the medication. The New Drug Application, or NDA, is based on the positive results from four trials or studies, one of which had patients on Relacorilant for up to six years. The NDA was filed on December 30 last year and on March 3 the FDA accepted the application. Note that this is the normal process for the FDA to first take about two months to accept the application and Relacorilant has a target date of December 30 this year for it to be approved or not.

The second biggest risk is Teva Pharmaceutical’s litigation to bring a generic version of Corcept’s Korlym medication to market. Corcept first sued Teva in 2018 for patent infringement and the latest ruling in early January 2024 was ruled in Teva’s favor, which Corcept appealed. Teva also sued Corcept in June last year accusing the company of monopolizing the market for mifepristone-based Korlym. There was an appellate court hearing on July 7 on the original case with one of the three judges saying towards the end of the hearing, “Don’t know how I’m going to decide.” One sell-side analyst believes a decision could be reached in the next month or two, before the FDA has decided on Relacorilant.

Besides having Relacorilant approved by the FDA, since it looks to be more effective than Corcept’s current Korlym, the new medication could largely shutout Teva from a large portion of the Cushing Syndrome market.

It does appear that Teva’s generic is only having minor impact to Corcept as Corcept raised prices by approximately 4% in August for its own generic and Corcept continues to provide the same level of support, no matter which version or Corcept’s medication the patient it is taking.

Chart shows the stock has been consolidating

Over almost five months Corcept’s shares have essentially been range bound between $65 and $75 until the stock crossed into the $80’s just over a week ago. This pattern is positive as there were multiple times the shares could have materially fallen after announcing disappointing financial results, but they did not.

What this all means

It certainly appears that there is potential for a significant upside to Corcept’s EPS results for the September quarter. If the company can deliver on the expected revenue projections, deliver EPS upside, maintain its $850 million to $900 million 2025 revenue guidance and reiterate the $3 billion to $5 billion in endocrinology revenue guidance between 2028 to 2030, I see very little downside to the shares and significant upside.

data.title: 2 Attractively Priced Closed-End Funds For Monthly Pay
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

In the world of income investing, several different options exist that an investor can choose. An investor can invest in individual investments such as higher-paying equity securities, common stocks that grow their dividends over time, or any number of fixed-income-oriented instruments. However, there are also closed-end funds and exchange-traded funds, which can provide immediate diversification and professional management.

With CEFs, an investor can have the opportunity to pick up investments at a discount relative to their net asset value—that's one of their most appealing features, as well as being able to invest in more complex and less liquid investments. They don't need to have the same liquidity requirements as ETFs or traditional mutual funds due to their "closed" structure.

Of course, like any investment choice, there are trade-offs no matter what one may choose. The higher diversification, professional management, and type of fund structure can come with higher expenses that will not be a consideration for an investor doing their own research. With that said, we are highlighting two closed-end funds today that are trading at historically attractive discounts and provide monthly distributions to their investors.

1. Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.24
  • Discount/Premium: -6.17%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.37%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.08%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.771 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund's (ETV) investment objective is to "provide current income and gains, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation." To achieve this, the fund will invest "in a diversified portfolio of common stocks and write call options on one or more U.S. indices on a substantial portion of the value of its common stock portfolio to seek to generate current earnings from the option premium."

The fund writes index options rather than on individual underlying holdings, with the fund generally close to being 100% overwritten at all times. That can mean their upside is capped when the S&P 500 Index and Nasdaq are rising sharply, given that their portfolio is concentrated in those same Mag 7 names that have the highest impact on those indexes overall.

With that said, the fund rolls the short call options every two weeks. That creates a situation where they can constantly readjust the percentage they are writing out-of-the-money.

This fund has been a rather long-time holding of mine and one I've added to regularly over time as well. The fund is currently once again presenting an attractive opportunity based on its discount being within our "Buy" range.

The fund had historically traded at a premium regularly, but after the tech-driven bear market of 2022, on the back of higher rates, the fund adjusted its distribution lower. Once that occurred, investors started to bail on the fund quite dramatically. That had been the first distribution cut since going back closer to the Global Financial Crisis, so it seemed to catch some investors off guard. Since then, they have bumped the payout up a touch—driven by the strong returns largely thanks to those Mag 7 tech names surging.

The underlying matters quite a bit for this fund's distribution because the payout will be funded primarily by capital gains. Those capital gains can come from the option writing strategy or the underlying appreciation in the portfolio. Either way, it makes the fund sensitive to the overall equity market direction as measured by the S&P 500 Index and Nasdaq Composite.

One of the more appealing aspects of ETV is the characterization of the distribution historically has seen a large component of return of capital. That can be beneficial to investors holding in a taxable account because ROC distributions reduce an investor's cost basis, and, therefore, it becomes a way to defer tax obligations.

However, it is important to consider that the classification of the distributions can change quite drastically from year to year. 2024 is one of those years when the fund saw most of its payout as capital gains rather than ROC distributions.

2. Flaherty & Crumrine Total Return Fund

  • 1-Year Z-score: 2.01
  • Discount/Premium: -6.62%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.58%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.34%
  • Leverage: 37.3%
  • Managed Assets: $317.5 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

Flaherty & Crumrine Total Return Fund's (FLC) objective is simple: "To provide its common shareholders with high current income." They also have a secondary investment objective of "capital appreciation," though preferreds and preferred funds typically fall short of this secondary goal, given that the underlying instruments aren't traditionally known to see a lot of appreciation.

For investors that aren't looking to add to a more tech-heavy allocation or covered call strategy, there is FLC, which is also attractively valued currently based on its historical discount. This fund is invested primarily in preferred securities but also contains other fixed-income-oriented exposure, such as contingent convertible securities.

In looking at the largest issuers that the fund is invested in, one might note the high number of financial institutions. That is certainly the case, as the fund has listed 54.9% in banks, 20.6% in insurance, and another 3.2% listed as simply "finance." This isn't that uncommon for preferred focused funds because financial institutions are the most common issuer of these securities.

We know that investments such as preferreds are interest rate sensitive, and that is the case for FLC, with a leverage-adjusted effective duration of 5.6%. That means with a higher rate environment, the fund saw some significant headwinds during the 2022/2023 period.

Additionally, the fund is also leveraged, and unlike many other closed-end funds that utilize borrowings, it did not hedge itself for a higher rate environment. That resulted in quite a headwind for the fund as it saw its borrowing costs rise sharply, eating into the fund's available net investment income. That then saw the distribution reduced quite materially. When including the interest expenses, the fund's total expense ratio came to 4.65%.

With little surprise, that combination meant weak results for FLC investors during this period, and that was exacerbated by the fund slipping from what was a fairly regular premium to a deep discount.

Some of that has reversed, given the new environment we have shifted into is an easing environment now. Lower short-term rates should ease the costs of their borrowings, which is why we've seen the trend return to a growing distribution now. Further, lower potential long-term rates or even stabilized rates from here should bode well for potentially seeing the underlying portfolio continue to recover from the drop it experienced.

We may not get back to the premium levels that the fund flirted with previously, but regardless, the environment is shifting in favor of this fund now.

data.title: Donegal Group: An Insurer Trading Below Its True Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I did not wake up thinking that I would be writing about an insurance stock today, but Donegal Group Inc. (NASDAQ:DGICA) caught my interest.

Donegal is a regional insurer that simply conducts its own business, and honestly, that makes it kind of interesting.

First of all, Donegal’s management has tightened its underwriting, exited weaker accounts, and is focused on retaining the most profitable policies.

It also fortified returns by reinvesting its portfolio in higher yields, which provided a sustained tailwind to earnings.

Therefore, profitability has been enhanced, book value has increased, and the company has demonstrated that it can compete.

Moreover, Donegal is trading at a discount compared to its sector median. Yet, its return profile is currently more robust than what the market is pricing in.

While costs remain high, the progress in fundamentals outweighs the concern.

Hence, I assign Donegal a Buy rating at this point.

Company Overview

Donegal Group is a regional property and casualty insurance holding company located in Pennsylvania. Its history dates back to 1889, but the holding company was established in 1986.

The company provides personal lines like auto, homeowners, renters, umbrella, and even boat insurance. It also offers business-related products such as businessowner packages, general liability, commercial auto, and workers' compensation.

Additionally, it caters to the farm owners, which fits its tradition and local presence. Its business strategy focuses on underwriting policy and earning investment income through the collected premiums.

In order to limit risk, it transfers part of the exposure by signing reinsurance agreements.

Rather than selling directly, Donegal depends on independent insurance agents with high community ties. This practice enables Donegal to compete with bigger carriers while maintaining a personal touch.

Interestingly, the company integrates public listing with its mutual insurance traditions.

Donegal Mutual Insurance Company still holds a substantial stake. It balances shareholder interests with a tradition of serving policyholders first.

Overall, Donegal blends steady insurance fundamentals with a community-based distribution policy.

Now that we have had a glance at the way the company works, it is time to dig into the numbers and evaluate how it is doing there.

Q2 2025 Results and Building Momentum

Donegal’s Q2 2025 shows how emphasizing quality over volume can transform financial performance.

Underwriting Discipline Starts to Pay Off: In the quarter, the company wrote $233.8 million in premiums, 5.4% lower compared to the same period last year.

Earned premiums, however, decreased by only 1.1% to $231.8 million, indicating that Donegal has managed to retain profitable business while relinquishing the weaker accounts.

This change quickly appeared in the underwriting performance. The combined ratio improved to 97.7% from 103%, bringing the company back to underwriting profitability. The loss ratio decreased to 65.1% from 70.6%, and approximately one percentage point of this improvement was due to a favourable reserve development.

Since the weather and large-fire losses remained almost unchanged compared to the previous year, the strong performance was not by luck but was a real indicator of operational improvement.

Meanwhile, the expense ratio remained close to 32.2%, which reflects the necessity to improve its efficiency in the long run.

Segment Trends and Longer-Term Shifts: The segment breakdown has provided a better understanding of where things are going well and where challenges remain.

Premiums on the commercial side rose 1.9% to $144.9 million, and the combined ratio has improved to 101% from 104.9%. Within that mix, commercial multi-peril and commercial auto both performed well with ratios under 100%, but workers' compensation remained high, and the "other" category remained low.

Personal lines were more shuffled. Premiums fell by 15.3% to $88.9 million, but the combined ratio narrowed sharply to 91.7% from 98.6%. Personal auto was the best with a standout 79.3% ratio, though homeowners remained dragging at 115.1%.

In general, it demonstrates that the cleanup strategy of management is effective, although there may still be certain problem areas that require time to resolve.

These short-term outcomes are more understandable in the context of the long-term trends at Donegal.

The policy counts have steadily decreased since a peak of 106,686 in 2021 to 92,334 by the end of 2024.

Meanwhile, the retention improved to 89.2%, and the renewal pricing grew by double digits in commercial lines and by approximately 6% in personal lines.

Simply, the company has opted to write fewer policies, and each one is more profitable with higher margins that generate more reliable earnings.

Stronger Investment Income, Cautious on Reserves: These underwriting activities, together with the investment strategy by Donegal, have developed a strong tailwind.

The company had approximately $1.4 billion of invested assets at the end of June 2025 that yielded 3.5% as opposed to 3.3% the previous year.

The net investment income increased by 13.3% to $12.5 million. More importantly, these reinvestments during the quarter were in securities yielding approximately 5.67%, more than two full percentage points above the assets that matured.

This change implies that the investment income will continue to increase as the portfolio matures into the high-yielding bonds.

Reserves also affected the results. Positive growth last year gave an earnings boost, which helped push the combined ratio to less than 100%.

Nevertheless, the trend of reserve movements has always been unstable across different lines, so it will be significant to monitor the pattern.

Altogether, all these factors contributed to a tangible leap in profitability.

Net income increased to $16.9 million compared to $4.2 million last year, and earnings per share rose to 46 cents.

The book value per share shot up by almost 15% to $16.62, indicating that the gains were directly transferred to shareholder equity.

At the moment, the figures indicate that Donegal has reestablished its foundation and is gradually making progress towards more sustainable performance.

Cheap Valuation, Stronger Returns

Donegal trades at valuation levels that appear too low relative to its current returns.

Donegal’s GAAP P/E is at 8.26x compared to a sector median of 13.44x, a discount of almost 40%. On a forward basis, the gap is narrowed, yet it remains near 20%.

Revenue-based multiples are even more impressive, with EV/Sales at 0.67x and Price/Sales at 0.68x versus the sector median of more than 3x. An EV/EBITDA of 6.26x compared to 11.15x represents a discount of 44%.

The only gap that is modest is on book value, with Donegal at 1.16x relative to the sector median of 1.28x, about a 9% discount.

Investors also receive a dividend yield of 3.67%, which is comfortably higher than the sector's 2.89%.

Combined, the above figures show that Donegal is priced like a weak-return insurer despite an implied ROE of approximately 14% relative to a sector average of around 9.5%.

The market's caution is due to a number of factors. The recent performance was secured by positive reserve releases and increased investment yields, both of which may vary on a yearly basis.

Donegal has a high expense ratio of over 32%, which is a structural disadvantage.

Nevertheless, underlying returns are even better than the valuation indicates, and the dividend gives a buffer as investors await evidence of consistency.

It makes the existing market skepticism appear overly cautious rather than progressive.

Intrinsic Value and Upside Potential

I used a residual-income model to measure Donegal's market pricing.

Based on a book value per share of $16.62, a 9.5% cost of equity, and a long-term earnings growth rate of 2%, the model yields a fair value of $20.59 in the base case (annualized EPS of $1.84).

The conservative case, where EPS drops to $1.50, results in $15.97.

The optimistic case, where EPS stands at $2.10, drives intrinsic value to $24.13.

The running sensitivities widen the range to be as low as $13 in more adverse assumptions, and as high as $33 in the most favorable ones.

In other words, the downside is likely to settle in the mid-teens if skepticism is justified, while the upside is in the high twenties or low thirties if profitability sustains.

That is what the opportunity is all about. A slight rerating to the sector's P/B ratio of 1.28x would give the stock approximately 10% upside, but a transition to the sector's P/E of 13.44x would provide 60% or more.

The indicator will be a sign that underwriting profitability is viable, expense ratios are on a downward trend, and performance is not too dependent on reserves and investment fluctuations.

The company does not have to be flawless to achieve a high valuation; it just has to demonstrate steady performance.

Currently, the market is appreciating Donegal as a low-multiple and high-yield insurer, but it provides above-average returns on equity.

Investors who do not dwell on the short-term noise and focus on core business can observe that the stock is undervalued and has attractive upside potential.

Potential Risks

First of all, there is a risk that the recent profitability of Donegal might not be sustainable.

A part of the company's earnings strength was related to the favorable reserve development and increased investment returns, and both of these are volatile by nature.

In case the claims trends deteriorate or the reserve releases are transformed to reserve charges, the company would soon lose the underwriting profits that have been maintaining its current performance.

Interestingly, this could potentially impact Donegal’s dividend payouts. The company has been consistent in paying dividends, but weaker profits can slow down the dividend momentum.

Second, the high expense ratio remains a structural weakness.

Donegal is less efficient than most of its peers at over 32%, and this restricts its capacity to increase margins even if its loss ratios improve.

Until the management finds methods to reduce costs or achieve greater operating leverage, profit will deteriorate, and the stock will continue to trade at lower multiples despite improved underwriting discipline.

Last but not least, there are external risks that may burden both operations and valuation.

Growth might cease if pricing power is constrained due to competitive forces or regulatory backlash. The increase in investment income is also pegged on the yields remaining supportive, which might reverse in case of a fall in the rates.

Takeaway

Everything considered, Donegal seems to be in a better position than the market gives it credit for.

It has tightened its underwriting, enhanced profitability, and steadily increased book value while maintaining a conservative balance sheet.

Meanwhile, its investment portfolio offers a significant tailwind that would help it to sustain its earnings in the near quarters.

Despite the presence of risks around expenses and reserve movements, the general trend points towards more stable performance.

Therefore, with shares trading far below industry averages, the present setup provides an attractive entry point.

data.title: The Next Big Theme: September 2025
data.description:
Autonomous & Electric Vehicles

Waymo Secures First New York City Permit as Its Rapid U.S. Expansion Continues

Alphabet-backed (GOOG)(GOOGL) Waymo received a permit to test up to eight self-driving cars in Manhattan and Downtown Brooklyn through September 2025, with potential for expansion. The permit is a significant milestone for autonomous vehicle (AV) testing with NYC one of the country’s most complex urban environments and governed by the nation’s strictest AV safety rules. Waymo brings extensive testing experience to NYC, having completed over 10 million autonomous rides across other major U.S. cities and boasting a strong safety track record. The permit builds on Waymo’s nationwide expansion efforts, following its launch in Austin and the growth of its San Francisco operations earlier this year. Waymo also plans to bring AVs to Atlanta, Miami, and Washington, D.C., and begin operations in Philadelphia to expand further into the Northeast market.1

Artificial Intelligence

Record AI Valuations Signal Investor Confidence in the Industry’s Continued Growth

OpenAI is in talks for a secondary stock sale that would value the company at a staggering $500 billion and make it the most valuable private startup company in the world.2 The valuation highlights OpenAI’s dominance in AI, driven by rapid revenue expansion and an aggressive acquisition strategy, which includes the recent $1.1 billion purchase of Statsig, still subject to regulatory approval, to bolster the development of OpenAI’s Applications business. OpenAI rival Anthropic completed a $13 billion Series F funding round, resulting in a $183 billion post-money valuation, nearly tripling Anthropic’s $61.5 billion valuation earlier this year. In just eight months, Anthropic’s run-rate revenue has surged from about $1 billion to $5 billion, fueled by enterprise adoption and the rapid growth of its Claude Code product. Both funding rounds signal sustained investor bets on AI’s transformative potential, equipping these firms with scale, innovation capacity, and strategic resources to shape the next wave of AI-driven enterprise and customer solutions.3

U.S. Infrastructure

Manufacturing and Innovation Get a Supercharge From Apple

​Apple (AAPL) added a fresh $100 billion to its American Manufacturing Program (AMP), bringing it to $600 billion. The newly launched program aims to bring advanced component production back to the United States and strengthen supplier networks nationwide. Spanning all 50 states, the AMP supports more than 450,000 supplier and partner jobs and plans for the direct hires of 20,000 U.S. workers, primarily in R&D, silicon engineering, software, AI, and machine learning. Through partnerships with various U.S.-based companies, key AMP initiatives include building iPhone and Apple Watch glass in Kentucky, expanding rare-earth magnet production in Texas, and laying the groundwork for an end-to-end American silicon supply chain, which is expected to produce 19 billion chips by the end of 2025. Apple is also working with Broadcom (AVGO) and GlobalFoundries (GFS) to develop and manufacture cellular semiconductor components for its 5G communications products in the United States.4 These initiatives will strengthen domestic manufacturing while reinforcing America’s high-tech infrastructure in hardware, design, and innovation.

Robotics & Artificial Intelligence

Nvidia’s New Robot “Brain” Signals a Breakthrough in Physical Automation

Nvidia (NVDA) unveiled its latest robotics chip module, Jetson AGX Thor, designed to serve as a robot’s brain and power the next wave of robotics and physical AI. Built on Nvidia’s most advanced Blackwell graphics processor units (GPUs), the same architecture used in its AI and computer gaming chips, the new module delivers up to 7.5 times the AI compute of its predecessor. This power enables the modules to run generative AI models that can interpret the world around them, which is essential for humanoid robots. Leading robotics and tech firms, including Amazon, Meta, Boston Dynamics, Agility Robotics, and Caterpillar, along with startup Figure, are already using it, while companies like John Deere and OpenAI are evaluating potential applications. By enabling real-time, intelligent interactions at the edge, Jetson Thor supports multiple generative AI models on one platform, making advanced autonomy achievable across industries such as manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, and healthcare.5

U.S. Electrification

Momentum Builds for Nuclear Power and Renewables Amid Data Center Boom

The U.S. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission recently approved a waiver request by utility NextEra to restart its Duane Arnold nuclear power plant in Iowa by year-end 2029. The decision comes amid surging electricity demand driven by data centers. The plant, which was decommissioned in 2020, could bring 600 megawatts (MW) of electricity-generating capacity back online.6 Constellation Energy is evaluating whether to add next-gen nuclear reactors to its Clinton Clean Energy Center in Illinois, which currently has 1,121MW of capacity and will supply Meta exclusively starting in 2027.7 These projects highlight how nuclear and renewable energy producers can be well positioned to benefit from rising electricity and data center demand as tech companies increasingly contract both energy sources to expand and diversify their clean energy portfolios. Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Google accounted for 43% of all clean power purchase agreements signed in 2024.8

Cloud Computing

More Multi-Billion-Dollar Cloud Deals as AI Demand Soars

Google secured a six-year, $10 billion-plus cloud computing agreement with Meta, its biggest cloud deal to date. Meta will use Google Cloud servers, storage, networking, and related infrastructure to support its rapidly expanding AI initiatives, including personal superintelligence.9 For Google, the pact adds momentum to its Cloud unit, which is working to gain ground on established rivals such as Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Microsoft Azure. Earlier this year, Google also won cloud business from OpenAI, which had previously relied heavily on Microsoft’s Azure infrastructure.10 More broadly, the agreement highlights the scope of AI compute demands, which have rivals collaborating. It also signals that the cloud is not just infrastructure; it’s the linchpin enabling enterprise AI ambitions at scale.
